response:
Looking backward at what these data points teach us, I see a pattern that recurs across organizational life cycles. The simultaneity of the contradictory signals, high NPS alongside rising churn, is not novel. It maps to a well-documented phenomenon that bears careful examination.

The lesson from similar situations: aggregate metrics are the last to show distress. Revenue lags satisfaction. Satisfaction lags engagement. Engagement lags perceived value. By the time churn hits 12%, the underlying value perception likely shifted 2-3 quarters earlier, meaning the root cause predates the feature acceleration and hiring surge.

This teaches us something about measurement timing. The company is looking at lagging indicators (churn) and contemporaneous indicators (NPS, features shipped) and trying to find causation. But the causal chain runs through leading indicators that apparently aren't being tracked: feature adoption rates, session depth, support ticket sentiment, and user workflow completion rates.

What pattern is repeating here? The "build more, faster" response to growth anxiety. When companies feel competitive pressure, the instinctive response is to ship more features. This pattern has been observed repeatedly across SaaS companies, and the post-mortem almost always reveals the same lesson: velocity without direction accelerates you toward the wrong destination.

The mental model that needs updating: feature count is not a proxy for product value. The company appears to have internalized the assumption that shipping more features equals creating more value. The churn data suggests this assumption has broken down, and may never have been valid.
